subject: One-handed weapons (rapiers, flails, morningstars, war picks)
method: full human/LLM read of transcript 066 (not the heuristic)
note: All 26 tiers below were confirmed to match the stored tierlists.json exactly. The 'why' field is new content. These compete with versatile weapons; rated by power when acquired. Morningstars carry Heart Stopper (a two-turn stun), the most powerful default weapon action; rapiers are the highest-damage finesse weapon.

Infernal Rapier.why: A +2 finesse rapier granting +1 spell save DC AND letting you attack with your spellcasting modifier — enabling spell/weapon hybrids (e.g. a Charisma paladin) to land attacks and Smites while maxing save DC; also a once/rest level-6 Planar Ally summon.

Duellist's Prerogative.why: A legendary +3 rapier with +1d4 necrotic, a bonus-action attack and +1 crit while your off-hand is empty, and — crucially — a SECOND reaction every turn; the extra reaction (Counterspell, opportunity attacks, etc.) makes it a build-defining weapon.

Flail of the Vortex.why: An exploit-only +3 flail with +1d8 lightning and an at-will action for +d10 and a DC13 stun; not intended to be obtainable, but if you grab it, it's a very powerful (if not wildly broken) stun weapon.

The Sacred Star.why: A +2 morningstar (Heart Stopper) with +1d4 radiant that applies Radiating Orb on every hit, turns undead, and has a once/rest AOE blind — probably the best debuffing weapon in the game, piling attack-roll penalties and stuns on enemies; underrated because it isn't a damage king.

+1 Rapier.why: The highest-damage finesse weapon early on, with no great competition in Act 1 — the natural pick for a Dexterity character using a one-handed weapon and shield, or a Bard/Rogue.

Defender Flail.why: A +1 flail granting +1 AC and -1 to all physical damage taken — its passives work from the unequipped slot, so an archer or tank stacks it for very high AC/damage reduction (which scales nonlinearly); enables several tanky builds.

Sword of Screams.why: A no-enhancement rapier with +1d4 psychic on every hit; one of the few finesse options in early Act 1, so a solid damage pick for shield/dual-wielder finesse characters (the psychic type is a mild downside until the Resonance Stone).

Flail of Ages.why: A +1 flail with a once/rest Elemental Rage (+1 enhancement, +1d4 of a chosen type, and an on-hit condition); choosing cold applies Chilled (→ freeze-stun with Wet), and stacking damage types inflicts multiple conditions per attack — great for condition/reverb builds.

Morningstar.why: Non-magical d8 carrying Heart Stopper — a two-turn stun (Chest Trauma) on a short-rest cooldown, the most powerful default weapon action; even the plain version is worth swapping to for one stun-critical encounter a day.

+1 Morningstar.why: A +1 morningstar; worth buying specifically for reliable access to Heart Stopper's two-turn stun in the early game, since the +1 makes the attack more likely to land.

Rupturing Blade.why: A +1 rapier with a once/rest +d6+proficiency fire and double bleed/burn saving throws (you take a little self-damage); a fine finesse option for a Rogue/Bard who lacks longsword proficiency, though Phalar Aluve usually outclasses it.

Twist of Fortune.why: A +1 morningstar (Heart Stopper stun) that rerolls 1s/2s on its own damage die and has Blood Money (damage scaling with the target's gold) — the reroll is negligible and Blood Money is mostly flavor/exploit, but the stun keeps it useful.

Pelorsun Blade.why: A +1 rapier with +1d4 radiant and passive advantage vs undead (works from the unequipped slot); arrives in Act 3 where undead are scarcer, but radiant damage on a finesse weapon suits a finesse radiating-orb paladin.

Deep Delver.why: A no-enhancement war pick applying Shattered (+1d4 from this weapon, no save) on hit; by now Elemental Weapon gives +1d4 on any weapon, so it's weak — but the guaranteed no-save condition can cheaply apply Radiating Orb/reverberation.

Harmonic Dueller.why: A +1 rapier with a once/rest performance check granting +Charisma to weapon damage for 10 turns; you can't pre-buff and swap away (the buff drops when unequipped), so only a niche dual-wield build wants it, and better options exist.

Ravengard's Scourger.why: A +2 morningstar (Heart Stopper) with a once/rest Commander's Strike; even the strong stun action can't save an otherwise generic +2 weapon with a weak, niche bonus-action ability in Act 3.

Flail.why: Non-magical d8 with a solid action set (Tenacity, Concussive Smash, Weakening Strike), but no character starts with one, so by the time you find it you already have magical weapons.

Rapier.why: Non-magical d8 finesse with good weapon actions (Piercing Strike, Weakening Strike, Flourish); a great chassis, but you can't start with one, so it's superseded by magical weapons immediately.

War Pick.why: Non-magical d8 with Piercing Strike, Weakening Strike, and a Maiming Strike (immobilize); a fine action set, but the rarest weapon type and never available before magical alternatives.

+1 Flail.why: A generic +1 flail; outclassed by other weapons available from the start of the game, so there's no reason to buy it.

+1 War Pick.why: A generic +1 war pick; like the +1 flail, it's outcompeted by better early options.

Corrosive Flail.why: A +1 flail with a once/rest acid-surface action (-2 enemy AC); only once/rest, you usually stand in your own acid (taking the penalty), and it requires hitting first — defeating the point of an accuracy debuff.

Hoppy.why: A +1 war pick with a once/rest +proficiency necrotic and 1d6 self-heal; far too minor an effect to justify the weapon slot, and other weapons heal you better.

Myrkulite Scourge.why: A +1 flail with +1d6 necrotic; its only advantage is bonus damage, but as a late Act 2 weapon it simply deals less than the alternatives you already have.

+2 Flail.why: A generic +2 flail only guaranteed at the very end; worse than the magical weapons you have by then.

+2 Rapier.why: A generic +2 rapier, same story as the +2 flail — outclassed by your existing weapons, so not worth it.
